results.question: What is impetigo in children?
results.reasoning: The context contains enough evidence from multiple sources about impetigo in children, including its causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment. The most relevant information found is that impetigo is a bacterial skin infection common among preschoolers and school-age kids, caused by Staphylococcus aureus or group A streptococcus bacteria, and can be contagious. It can cause blisters or sores, but is not usually serious and improves with antibiotic treatment. There are gaps in the information about the specific age range of children most affected, but overall, the context provides a good understanding of impetigo in children. The question is clearly in-scope as it pertains to pediatric health.
results.final_answer: Impetigo is a very common bacterial skin infection among kids, especially preschoolers and school-age kids. It can cause blisters or sores on the skin, but is not usually serious and improves with antibiotic treatment. Impetigo is contagious and can spread to anyone who touches infected skin or items that have touched infected skin.

retrieved_docs.title: Impetigo | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
Impetigo
What Is Impetigo?
Impetigo (im-peh-TY-go) is a very common bacterial skin infection among kids, especially preschoolers and school-age kids. It can cause blisters or sores, is not usually serious, and improves with antibiotic treatment.
What Causes Impetigo?
Two types of bacteria — Staphylococcus aureus and group A streptococcus (which also causes strep throat) — usually cause impetigo. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is also becoming an important cause of impetigo.

retrieved_docs.title: Staph Infections | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content: - A furuncle (fyoor-UNK-ul), commonly known as a boil: These swollen, red, painful lumps in the skin usually are due to an infected hair follicle. The lump fills with pus, growing larger and more painful until it ruptures and drains. Furuncles often begin as folliculitis and then worsen. They most often appear on the face, neck, buttocks, armpits, and inner thighs, where small hairs can get irritated. A cluster of several furuncles is called a carbuncle (KAR-bunk-ul). retrieved_docs.title: Impetigo | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
The three types of impetigo are non-bullous (crusted), bullous (large blisters), and ecthyma (ulcers):
- Non-bullous or crusted impetigo is most common. It begins as tiny blisters that eventually burst and leave small wet patches of red skin that may weep fluid. Gradually, a yellowish-brown or tan crust covers the area, making it look like it has been coated with honey or brown sugar.
- Bullous impetigo causes larger fluid-containing blisters that look clear, then cloudy. These blisters are more

retrieved_docs.title: Impetigo | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content: Kids can be more likely to develop impetigo when their skin is already irritated by another problem, such as eczema, poison ivy, insect bites, and cuts or scrapes. Bacteria that usually live harmlessly on the skin can get into the body through the area of broken skin, leading to symptoms of impetigo. Scratching a sore or a rash is a common cause — for example, poison ivy can get infected and turn into impetigo. It also happens more often in warm, humid environments. Making sure that kids wash th

retrieved_docs.title: Impetigo | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
How Is Impetigo Diagnosed?
In most cases, doctors can diagnose impetigo based on how the rash looks. Occasionally, they may need to take a sample of fluid from blisters for testing.
How Is Impetigo Treated?
Impetigo is typically treated with antibiotics, either as a cream/ointment to put on skin or a medicine taken by mouth:
- When it just affects a small area of the skin (and especially if it's the non-bullous form), impetigo is treated with antibiotic ointment for 5 days.
- If the infection ha

results.question: Quais são os sintomas da caxumba em crianças?
results.reasoning: O contexto contém evidências suficientes para responder à pergunta. As principais informações encontradas incluem que os sintomas de mumps podem variar desde nenhum sintoma até sintomas leves como um resfriado, e em casos mais graves, a inflamação dos glândulas parotídeas pode causar dor e dificuldade para engolir. Além disso, é importante notar que as crianças vacinadas podem ainda desenvolver mumps, mas com sintomas menos graves do que se não tivessem sido vacinadas.
results.final_answer: Os sintomas de mumps em crianças podem variar desde nenhum sintoma até sintomas leves como um resfriado. Em casos mais graves, a inflamação dos glândulas parotídeas pode causar dor e dificuldade para engolir. Além disso, é importante notar que as crianças vacinadas podem ainda desenvolver mumps, mas com sintomas menos graves do que se não tivessem sido vacinadas.

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
Mumps
What Is Mumps?
Mumps is an infection caused by a virus. It can infect many parts of the body, but is best known for causing swelling of the parotid glands. These glands, which make saliva (spit), are in front of the ear, around the jaw.
Mumps used to be a common childhood illness in the United States, especially in kids 5 to 9 years old. It's much rarer now, thanks to the mumps vaccine.
What Are the Signs & Symptoms of Mumps?
Many kids have no symptoms, or very mild symptoms that feel like

- has a high fever or one that lasts more than a few days
- has breathing problems
- is under 3 months old or has a weak immune system
- has red eyes, eye pain, or a change in vision
- has severe diarrhea, vomiting, or signs of dehydration, such as peeing less or having fewer wet diapers, a dry mouth, sunken eyes, or acting tired and listless
You know your child best. If your child seems very ill, call your doctor right away.

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
Sometimes people who have been vaccinated still get mumps. But their symptoms will be much milder than if they had not gotten the vaccine.
During a mumps outbreak, doctors may recommend more shots of the MMR vaccine for some people who are more likely to get mumps. Your doctor will have the most current information.
When Should I Call the Doctor?
Mumps can sometimes cause rare but severe problems. Call the doctor right away if your child has mumps and:
- gets a severe headache
- has a stiff neck

Someone who also has angioedema might have puffiness, blotchy redness, swelling, or large bumps around the eyes, lips, hands, feet, genitals, or throat. Other symptoms can include nausea, vomiting, or belly pain.
Rarely, a person with hives and angioedema can also get anaphylactic shock. Signs of anaphylactic shock include breathing trouble, a drop in blood pressure,

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
- Soothe swollen parotid glands with either warm or cool compresses (whichever feels better).
- Help your child get plenty of rest.
- Kids with mumps should stay home for 5 days from the start of parotid gland swelling. Ask your doctor about when your child can return to school or childcare.
Mumps is caused by a virus, so it can't be treated with antibiotics. Antibiotics work only against bacteria.
How Long Does Mumps Last?
Most children with mumps recover fully in about 2 weeks.

results.question: What are the symptoms of chickenpox in children?
results.reasoning: The context contains enough evidence to answer the question as it provides detailed information on the signs and symptoms of chickenpox from multiple reliable sources. The most relevant pieces of information found include early symptoms such as headache, poor appetite, sore throat, stomachache, or fever, followed by a red, itchy skin rash that spreads throughout the body in three stages: red bumps, blisters, and scabs. However, there are gaps or uncertainties regarding the severity of symptoms in children with weak immune systems or skin disorders like eczema. The question is in-scope as it pertains to pediatric health-related information.
results.final_answer: The symptoms of chickenpox in children often start with a fever or headache, followed by an itchy full-body rash that progresses through three stages: red bumps, blisters filled with fluid, and scabs. These symptoms can appear anywhere on the body, including the scalp, mouth, arms, legs, and genitals. In some cases, chickenpox can cause more severe problems in children with weak immune systems or skin disorders like eczema.

retrieved_docs.title: Chickenpox (Varicella) | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
- The chickenpox vaccine protects most kids from getting sick or makes symptoms much milder.
Signs & Symptoms
What Are the Signs & Symptoms of Chickenpox?
Early Symptoms
Chickenpox often starts with a headache, poor appetite, sore throat, stomachache, or fever. These symptoms may last for a few days.
Chickenpox Rash
A red, itchy skin rash usually starts on the belly or back and face. Then it spreads to almost everywhere else on the body, including the scalp, mouth, arms, legs, and genitals.
The

retrieved_docs.title: Chickenpox (Varicella) | Nemours KidsHealth
retrieved_docs.content:
The rash may spread wider or be more severe in kids who have weak immune systems or skin disorders like eczema.
When Should I Call the Doctor?
Most chickenpox infections don't need special medical treatment. But sometimes, problems can happen.
Get medical attention right away if your child:
- has trouble breathing
- has a severe headache
- is very drowsy or has trouble waking up
- has trouble looking at bright lights
- has trouble walking
- seems confused
- seems very ill
- has a stiff neck
Call

Chickenpox: Treatment & Care
Chickenpox (varicella) infection is much less common in the United States than it used to be, thanks to the chickenpox vaccine. If someone does get the infection, the rash and other symptoms it causes will go away without treatment. Chickenpox is usually not a big deal, but it can sometimes cause serious problems.
What Are the Signs & Symptoms of Chickenpox?
The classic chickenpox rash has three stages and all can appear on the body at the same time:

Chickenpox (Varicella)
Overview
What Is Chickenpox?
Chickenpox is an infection that causes a fever and an itchy rash with spots all over the body. It’s due to a virus called varicella-zoster. Chickenpox used to be a common childhood illness in the United States, especially in kids under age 12. It's much rarer now, thanks to the chickenpox vaccine, which helps prevent the infection.
